data.title: Small, midcap indices jump 2% on US tariff reprieve: Here are the top gainers
data.description:
Broader markets

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

The shares of smallcap and midcap companies recorded strong gains on April 11 as markets found some relief after US President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause in his reciprocal tariffs. The Nifty Smallcap index was up nearly 2.3 percent to stand at 15,605, while the Nifty Midcap index was hovering 1.6 percent higher at 50,386 in morning trade.

US President Donald Trump had announced harsh reciprocal tariffs on April 2, sending global markets sharply lower. Indian stock markets also crashed, with the smallcap and midcap indices recording significant losses as well. Later on April 9, Trump announced a 90-day pause to his tariffs on several nations barring China. Global markets positively reacted to the news, with Indian markets being no exception. Sensex gained over 1,300 points, while Nifty jumped nearly 2 percent. The sharp recovery in the benchmark indices also spilled over to the broader markets.

Inox Wind shares were the top gainer on the smallcap index, rising over 7 percent to trade at Rs 151 apiece. Amber Enterprises and PG Electroplast shares followed, rising over 6 percent each. Other notable smallcap stocks which recorded massive gains include PVR Inox (up nearly 6 percent), Laurus Labs (up over 5 percent), Kaynes Tech (up over 4 percent), MCX (up over 4 percent), Nuvama (up nearly 4 percent), PNB Housing Finance (up nearly 4 percent), FirstCry (up over 3 percent), Titagarh Rail (up nearly 3 percent), Angel One (up over 2 percent), IRCON (up nearly 2 percent) and others.

On the midcap index, Dixon Tech shares were the top gainer. The shares of the company surged over 7 percent to trade at Rs 14,237 apiece. Other notable midcap stocks which strongly gained include Bharti Hexacom (up nearly 5 percent), Oberoi Realty (up nearly 5 percent), Policy Bazaar (up nearly 5 percent), Cochin Shipyard (up nearly 4 percent), Paytm (up nearly 4 percent), Motilal Oswal Financial Services (up nearly 4 percent), SAIL (up nearly 4 percent), Patanjali Foods (up nearly 4 percent), Biocon (up over 3 percent), Mphasis (up over 3 percent), Bharat Dynamics (up over 3 percent), Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders (up over 3 percent), Polycab (up over 2 percent), Suzlon Energy (up over 2 percent), IRCTC (up over 2 percent) and more.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Muthoot Finance tumbles 6% as RBI’s draft gold loan norms spark volatility; Kotak downgrades stock
data.description:
Muthoot Finance tumbles 6% as RBI’s draft gold loan norms spark volatility; Kotak downgrades stock

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Shares of Muthoot Finance slumped over 6 percent on Friday, April 11, following the Reserve Bank of India’s release of draft guidelines on gold-backed loans—a move that spooked investors and triggered sharp intraday volatility and a surge in trading volumes. The sell-off was sharp enough to briefly push the stock into a 10% lower circuit before a partial recovery.

The draft norms propose capping the Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio at 75 percent, restricting bullet repayment loan tenures to 12 months, and mandating NBFCs to set limits on their gold loan portfolios. While RBI Governor Sanjay Malhotra later clarified the intent was to “rationalise” norms rather than tighten them, the street reaction remained nervous.

Kotak Institutional Equities downgraded Muthoot Finance to ‘Add’ from ‘Buy’, slashing its target price to Rs 2,250 from Rs 2,400 and cutting earnings estimates by 10%. The brokerage said, “While we await final norms, growth and margin assumptions have been moderated. The regulatory overhang will temper stock price performance.”

Axis Securities flagged the intensity of the sell-off, noting a 7 percent intraday decline and the highest single-day volume since January 2024. Open interest in futures surged by 20 percent, with 7.8 lakh shares added—marking the largest single-day build-up in a year and suggesting a heavy short build-up. The options market echoed a bearish tone with aggressive call writing.

Jefferies acknowledged some impact from the tighter LTV norms but noted the proposed rollover norms weren’t as severe as feared. The firm maintained a constructive stance on gold-loan NBFCs, naming Muthoot Finance as its preferred pick with a target of Rs 2,615.

CLSA viewed the guidelines as “marginally disruptive” to growth, building in 12–15% loan growth for Muthoot and Manappuram over FY26–27. Morgan Stanley, meanwhile, kept an ‘Equalweight’ on Muthoot, warning of near-term pressure, and said Manappuram could be better placed, citing Bain Capital’s open offer.

Muthoot’s peers saw mixed action: IIFL Finance was trading 1.1 percent lower, while Manappuram Finance was trading 0.8 percent higher. Manappuram remains under the F&O ban, preventing new positions.

While the final RBI rules will determine the long-term impact, Friday’s action reflected a nervous readjustment by the market, with technicals and sentiment turning negative for Muthoot in the short term.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Investors cheer Trump's tariff pause: These are the top gaining spaces
data.description:
Market

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Benchmark Indian indices surged on April 11, with Sensex rising over 1,300 points and Nifty 50 up nearly 2 percent near 22,800 following a relief rally on US President Trump's 90-day pause on tariffs, which is expected to provide relief to investors with exposure to external-oriented spaces.

Here are the top gaining sectors on Dalal Street following the tariff pause:

Pharma

While Trump has not unveiled any specific plan related to the tariffs on pharma sector, the overall pause and ongoing talks about a US-India bilateral trade agreement (BTA) will likely benefit the sector. The Nifty Pharma index was up over 2 percent to 20,450. Granules shares were the top gainer on the index, rising nearly 5 percent. Other notable stocks include Cipla (up nearly 4 percent), Laurus Labs (up nearly 4 percent), Biocon (up over 3 percent), Dr Reddy’s Laboratories (up over 2 percent) and Sun Pharma (up over 2 percent).

IT

Most of the IT players derive major portions of their revenue from the US. Trump's tariffs triggered fears of US recession, which acted as a major negative for the stocks. However, after the pause on the tariffs, Goldman Sachs dropped their US recession forecast. This acted as a major positive for the sector. Coforge shares were the top gainer on the index in the morning, rising nearly 4 percent to trade at Rs 6,571 apiece. LTI MidnTree, Mphasis and Wipro shares followed, trading over 2 percent each. Infosys, HCL Tech and Persistent shares meanwhile gained nearly 2 percent each.

Metals

The Nifty Metal index was the top gaining index on the stock market, rising nearly 4 percent to hover around 8,143. Tata Steel, which saw a strong slump after Trump’s reciprocal tariff plan, surged nearly 5 percent to trade at Rs 133 apiece. Other notable stocks include JSW Steel (up over 4 percent), Adani Enterprises  (up over 4 percent), Hindalco (up over 4 percent), SAIL (up nearly 4 percent) and Vedanta (up over 3 percent).

Auto Component

The shares of auto component makers have surged as these companies have large exposure to the US market. Sona BLW shares were up 4 percent, while those of Samvardhana Motherson were trading nearly 3 percent higher.

Aquaculture

The shares of shrimp exporting companies also surged as these have strong export exposure to US, with significant portion of their revenue being driven by the American market. Notable stocks include Avanti Feeds (up over 8 percent), Apex Frozen Foods (up nearly 6 percent) and Coastal Corp (up nearly 6 percent).

Electronic Components

Stocks of electronics manufacturing services (EMS) firms in India saw a sharp uptick as global brands like Apple may continue to source parts from India following the tariff pause. Notably, the shares were already on the rise since reports emerged stating that Apple is planning to source more iPhones from India to the US to beat the tariffs imposed on China. Kaynes Tech shares were up nearly 4 percent, while PG Electroplast shares jumped over 6 percent. Dixon Tech shares also surged over 6 percent.

Textile 

Indian textile players are expected may benefit from the 145 percent tariff imposed on China, which is the biggest supplier of textiles and apparel to the US. Prominent stocks include Grasim Industries (up nearly 3 percent), Trident (up 1.3 percent), Welspun Living (up over 4 percent) and Vardhman Textiles (up 1.3 percent).

Solar EPC 

India's solar engineering, procurement and construction (EPC) firms have significant exposure to the US market, and the tariff pause is set to benefit these companies. Premier Energies shares gained over 2 percent, while Waaree Energies shares were trading with marginal gains.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Tata Steel, JSW, SAIL, other metal stocks rise up to 7% as tariff war fears subside after 90-day pause
data.description:
Metal stocks rise up to 7% as tariff war fears subside after 90-day pause by the United States. 

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Metal shares were the flavour today, emerging as the top sectoral gainer as the global tariff war fears subsided after the US President Donald Trump announced a 90-day pause. Additionally, Tata Steel's news to transform its Netherlands operations also added positivity to the momentum in the index.

The Nifty Metal index climbed 4.44 percent - its best gain since March 5, 2025 when the index advanced over 4 percent. Lloyds Metals And Energy was top gainer on the index, rising 6.81 percent to an intraday high of Rs 1,230 per share on the NSE.

Tata Steel followed closely, gaining 5.91 percent to a high of Rs 134.7 on the back of its transformation plans in Netherlands.

Ajit Mishra, Senior Vice President at Religare Broking, noted "A relief recovery in metals alongside with other key sectors was witnessed, in response to the deferred tariff news. Besides, Tata Steel's news to transform its Netherlands operations is also adding to the positivity."

Tata Steel Nederland will slash around 1,600 jobs in management and support functions as part of a sweeping transformation plan aimed at improving efficiency and preparing for a green steel future.

“This transformation is a building block towards the future,” said T. V. Narendran, CEO and MD of Tata Steel and Chairman of the Supervisory Board of Tata Steel Nederland. “We want to ensure Tata Steel Nederland regains its position as one of the most efficient steelmakers in Europe while taking definitive steps toward sustainability.”

The company believes these changes will strengthen its operating and financial profile, enabling it to invest in the future and remain competitive in a rapidly evolving industry.

Tata Steel to transform operations in Netherlands, 1,600 jobs to disappear

data.title: TCS shares in mild green post-Q4 results; brokerages weigh near-term risks, reiterate long-term optimism
data.description:
TCS shares in focus post Q4 miss; brokerages weigh near-term risks, reiterate long-term optimism

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Shares of Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) are under focus for Friday's trade after the IT major’s March quarter (Q4FY25) results failed to meet Street expectations on both revenue and margins. The stock, which closed flat in the previous session, saw muted reactions early in the day with a subtle gain following brokerage reactions as they digested the miss but largely reiterated their bullish stance, citing deal wins and improving visibility for FY26.

Muted Q4, but deal pipeline offers comfort

TCS reported revenue of $7.5 billion in Q4FY25, down 1 percent sequentially in USD terms, slightly worse than estimates. EBIT margin fell 30 basis points QoQ to 24.2 percent, also below expectations. Net profit declined 1.3 percent QoQ to Rs 12,200 crore. Full-year revenue stood at $30.2 billion, up 4.2 percent in constant currency (CC) terms, while the FY25 total contract value (TCV) came in at $39.4 billion. The Q4 deal TCV rose 20 percent sequentially to $12.2 billion, although it was down 8 percent YoY.

The pain point in Q4 was the BSNL deal ramp-down, which dragged regional market revenues lower by 6 percent QoQ. India revenues were down 15% QoQ, while the US was flat, and Europe grew 2 percent. BFSI grew 1.3 percent QoQ and remains a key driver going forward.

Motilal Oswal: "Revenue risks remain, but margin recovery could support earnings"
Motilal Oswal flagged the near-term uncertainty, especially with the BSNL drag and soft discretionary spending. “The base case is that the current uncertainty will dissipate, but we remain cautious. We expect Q1 to be a QoQ decline, including BSNL,” it said. While margins were a disappointment, the firm expects FY26 EBIT margin to improve to 25.3 percent aided by cost reductions. Despite macro risks, the strong deal TCV and margin levers prompted the brokerage to retain its 'Buy' rating with a target price of Rs 3,850.

Antique Broking: "Upgrade to BUY on inexpensive valuation"
Antique upgraded TCS from Hold to Buy, citing attractive valuations after a 30 percent correction from its peak. “We believe the current price offers a 28 percent upside, and with a gradual recovery expected from 2HFY26, the risk-reward is favourable,” it said. The brokerage maintained its FY26 outlook, noting that client budgets were flat but not cut, with discretionary spend merely postponed. Its target price of Rs 4,150 is based on 25x FY27 earnings.

HDFC Securities: "Strong deal wins, near-term delays manageable"
HDFC Securities also maintained a 'Buy' rating with a target price of Rs4,000, highlighting the broad-based nature of the Q4 deal wins. It noted challenges in verticals like Retail & CPG and Manufacturing but flagged no impact on BFSI and called the pause in discretionary spending "short-lived". It expects EPS to grow at a CAGR of 9 percent between FY25-27, with EBIT margins improving to 25.6 percent by FY27.

Prabhudas Lilladher: "TCS well-positioned to ride enterprise tech spends"
Prabhudas Lilladher held on to its 'Buy' rating with a higher target price of Rs 4,810, assigning a 28x FY27 PE multiple. The brokerage expects margin expansion in FY26 on the back of the BSNL taper and stronger core geography contribution. “TCS is well positioned to capture enterprise tech spends with its full-stack offerings,” it said, projecting a 13.1 percent earnings CAGR over FY25-27.

Centrum: "Muted near-term, but recovery likely as GenAI scales"
Centrum noted the Q4 performance was mixed, with both revenue and margins below estimates. However, the record Q4 deal book of $12.2 billion and expected ramp-up of GenAI initiatives could drive recovery. It revised earnings estimates lower but upgraded the stock to 'Buy', setting a target of Rs 4,211, implying a 30 percent upside.

Out of the 48 analysts covering the stock, 35 have iterated a 'Buy' while 10 have called for a 'Hold' and only three have rated it as a 'Sell'.

data.title: Pharma stocks rally on Trump's tariff pause, led by up to 5% surge in Granules, Laurus Labs, Cipla
data.description:
Pharma stocks

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

The shares of pharma companies surged on April 11 after US President Trump paused his reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, easing concerns over a possible US recession, and helped by the anticipation that the pharma sector is unlikely to bear the brunt of major tariff levies.

The rise in the stock prices pushed Nifty Pharma index up nearly 3 percent in the early trade.

Granules shares were the top gainer on the index, rising nearly 5 percent to trade at Rs 453 apiece. Laurus Labs and Cipla shares were meanwhile up over 4 percent each. Sun Pharma, Aurobindo Pharma and Biocon jumped over 3.7 percent each.

Lupin and Zydus Life shares gained nearly 3 percent each, while Natco Pharma, Dr Reddy’s Labs, Gland Pharma and Torrent Pharma were up nearly 2 percent each. Mankind Pharma, Abbott India, Zydus Lifesciences and others were trading over a percent higher.

Trump had on April 2 unveiled a slew of reciprocal tariffs on trading partners, triggering a global markets selloff, however, he chose to keep pharma sector out of the purview, but later hinted at including the drugmakers too in the ambit.

N Jayakumar, the MD of Prime Securities told CNBC Awaaz that the pharma sector is an opportunity to buy, and Trump's threat of tariff on drug makers is more aimed at the Big Pharma based out of Europe, and the dumping into US by China. Prashant Khemka, founder and MD of WhiteOak Capital said the tariff turmoil has meant that volatility is now a part and parcel of the game. He too concurred that the tariff threat on Pharma is only a negotiation ploy, and Indian companies would be relatively better off.

Also Read: 3 reasons why Trump announced 90-day pause on tariffs

On April 9, Trump had paused his reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, stating that several nations are trying to negotiate a deal with US. Moneycontrol reported on April 10 that India is in talks with the US for a bilateral trade agreement (BTA), and a partial deal may be sealed soon. While no specifics have been given for pharma stocks, the tariff pause and the India-US trade talks are being seen as a positive trigger for the sector.

Rahul Singh, CIO - Equities at Tata Asset Management said, "The recent global tariff changes have added uncertainty to markets, but India is better positioned than before. With lower exposure to US trade and strong domestic demand, the direct impact remains manageable. In fact, falling global prices of crude and metals may support Indian companies by reducing input costs. Sectors like banking, pharma, and energy now offer attractive value with solid balance sheets."

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: Coforge, Wipro, Infosys lead Nifty IT rebound as Trump's tariff pause eases business jitters
data.description:
IT stocks

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Set Alert
 
 
live
  • bselive
  • nselive
Volume
Todays L/H
More

Shares of IT companies surged on April 11 after President Trump paused his tariffs on trading partners for 90 days, easing concerns over a potential US recession and possible delays in clients' IT budgets, with reports suggesting India could look at this window to seal a partial trade deal with Trump.

The rise in the stock prices pushed the Nifty IT index higher by nearly 1.3 percent in early trade.

Coforge shares were the top gainer on the index in the morning, rising nearly 4 percent to trade at Rs 6,571 apiece. LTI MidnTree, Mphasis and Wipro shares followed, trading over 2 percent each. Infosys, HCL Tech and Persistent shares meanwhile gained nearly 2 percent each.

TCS shares however bucked the trend to trade in the red with marginal losses at Rs 3,215 apiece. The fall in the stock price comes after the company posted a net profit of Rs 12,224 crore in Q4 FY25, missing estimate. This marked a fall of nearly 2 percent from the net profit reported in Q4 FY24.

The IT sector has been one of the worst-hit in this global market rout as these companies derive major portions of their revenue from the American market. The Nifty IT index crashed over 10 percent between April 2 and April 9.

However, Trump announced the temporary pause on tariffs on April 9, providing much needed relief to Wall Street, and also eased recession fears, with Goldman Sachs rescinding its recession prediction, a move that is seen as a positive cue for the IT sector.

Trump had unveiled a slew of reciprocal tariffs on April 2, with India being charged 26 percent tariff on its US exports. This sent global markets to a spin, with Indian stock markets being no exception. Apart from a global economic slowdown, the tariffs also increased concerns over a possible US recession. Goldman Sachs had earlier predicted 65 percent chance of the US going into a recession in the next 12 months.

data.title: Sensex up 1,100 pts, Nifty near 22,800: Trump tariff relief among key factors behind market rally today
data.description:
Benchmark equity indices soared sharply on Friday, led by firm global cues and renewed optimism around a possible India-US trade agreement, after Washington announced a 90-day suspension of the additional reciprocal tariffs.

Sensex opened with a strong gap-up, jumping 1,194.36 points or 1.61 percent to touch an early high of 75,041.51. The NSE Nifty also surged 383.9 points or 1.71 percent to scale 22,783.05 in early trade.

Market participants said the rally came on the back of positive momentum from global peers and a catch-up effect, as Indian markets were shut on Thursday for Mahavir Jayanti.

Asian equities had already rallied in the previous session, with Japan’s Nikkei index climbing 9.1 percent and Taiwan’s benchmark rising 9.3 percent.

Key factors behind market rise today: 

1) US suspends fresh tariffs on India, others: Sentiment was lifted after the United States temporarily suspended additional tariffs of up to 26 percent for a period of 90 days, until July 9. The decision, announced via an executive order from the White House, follows US President Donald Trump’s move on April 2 to impose sweeping universal tariffs on nearly 60 countries.

While the baseline 10 percent duty remains, the three-month pause provides crucial breathing space for Indian exporters and policymakers to push for a broader trade agreement with the US.

2) Trade deal push gains momentum: The temporary relief is expected to give momentum to ongoing discussions around a bilateral trade agreement (BTA) between the two countries. According to a report by Moneycontrol, New Delhi is working towards a partial BTA with Washington before the 90-day window expires.

The government is reportedly pursuing a three-pronged strategy aimed at deepening trade relations, enhancing market access, and safeguarding domestic industries.

3) India gains edge over regional peers: Unlike India, others including China, Hong Kong and Macau, have not been granted the tariff pause. This could offer Indian exporters a competitive advantage in the short term.

Technical Analysts

Commenting on the market outlook, Anand James, Chief Market Strategist at Geojit Financial Services, said, “Volatility is expected to remain elevated as the market digests the news flow post the mid-week holiday. While our earlier view was that markets could attempt another upswing, a clear upside target was avoided as the India VIX stayed above 21.”

He added that a break above 23,140 on the Nifty could trigger a fresh wave of buying. “If the index floats above 22,850, strength may continue. However, a dip towards 22,500 may attract renewed buying interest. Any weakness below 22,350 could drag the market, though a fall below 22,160 appears unlikely for now,” James said.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
